relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: A death transfers Ammonite power to Hanun and opens a vulnerable diplomatic moment.
David sends comfort in memory of an earlier kindness between rulers.
Hanun's advisers interpret condolence as reconnaissance and imagine overthrow behind care.
The envoys are physically exposed and marked through beard-shaving and torn clothing.
David gives the humiliated men distance and time rather than forcing public return.
Fear of retaliation leads Ammon to purchase a multinational military coalition.
David responds by sending Joab and the professional force.
The opposing armies divide between the city gate and open field.
Joab recognizes encirclement and deploys selected troops toward the Aramean line.
Abishai receives command against the Ammonite formation.
The brothers promise reciprocal rescue if either line fails.
Courage is invoked for people and cities, yet even sacred language cannot make war harmless.
The Aramean formation retreats before Joab.
Ammonite troops withdraw into the city, ending this stage without resolution.
The defeated coalition reorganizes rather than accepting the first retreat.
Hadadezer expands the war by drawing forces from beyond the River.
David personally mobilizes the wider kingdom and crosses Jordan.
The battle ends in immense casualties and the death of Shobach.
Peace arrives through defeat, fear, and vassal service—not restored trust.

contemplative_note: A diplomatic misunderstanding becomes a machinery of humiliation, fear, contracts, armies, casualties, and submission. The contemplative interruption is the moment before escalation, when suspicion can still be tested and exposed bodies can be met with dignity rather than vengeance.
